article_title: NASA JPL Fires Up 120-Kilowatt Lithium-Fed Magnetoplasmadynamic Thruster, a 25x Power Leap Over the Psyche Engines
summary: Editorially clean. Allowlist warning dismissed — spacewar.com is a recognized space-news outlet and the specific claims attributed to it are corroborated across the four other snapshots.

content_preview.title: NASA JPL Fires Up 120-Kilowatt Lithium-Fed Magnetoplasmadynamic Thruster, a 25x Power Leap Over the Psyche Engines
content_preview.summary: On Feb. 24, a JPL-led team ran a lithium-vapor magnetoplasmadynamic thruster at 120 kilowatts inside the CoMeT vacuum chamber, more than 25 times the power of the electric engines on NASA's Psyche spacecraft.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

On Feb. 24, engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California fired up a prototype electric thruster that runs on lithium metal vapor and reached power levels of up to 120 kilowatts, [according to JPL](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasa-fires-up-powerful-lithium-fed-thruster-for-trips-to-mars/). The agency disclosed the test results publicly in late April and early May, calling it the first time in years that an electromagnetic thruster of this design had operated...

editor_notes.content_quality: Solid 769-word News piece. Standard Machine Herald structure (Overview, What We Know, Why Lithium and Why MPD, What's Next, What We Don't Know). Technical handling (MPD architecture, lithium vapor propellant, CoMeT chamber rationale, tungsten electrode at 5,000°F) competent for a general technical readership.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 5 snapshots. Every direct quote verified verbatim and correctly attributed: Polk 'Designing and building these thrusters over the last couple of years has been a long lead-up to this first test' + 'It's a huge moment for us because we not only showed the thruster works, but we also hit the power levels we were targeting' (all 5 sources); Isaacman 'At NASA, we work on many things at once, and we haven't lost sight of Mars' (all 5 sources); Isaacman 'This marks the first time in the United States that an electric propulsion system has operated at power levels this high, reaching up to 120 kilowatts' (all 5 sources). Technical specifics confirmed across multiple snapshots: Feb 24 test, 120 kW, CoMeT chamber, 26-foot water-cooled vacuum chamber, tungsten electrode at 5,000°F / 2,800°C, 25× Psyche power, Princeton + Glenn Research Center collaboration, NASA's Space Nuclear Propulsion office, 90% less propellant than chemical rockets, 2–4 megawatts crewed-Mars target, 23,000-hour operating requirement, 500 kW–1 MW per-thruster goal, two-and-a-half-year development timeline.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specifics verified. No fabrications. The 'first time in years' framing of the JPL press release is correctly carried through.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and properly hedged. 'What We Don't Know' section accurately flags the missing specific-impulse / efficiency numbers, the unbuilt nuclear power source, and the durability gap (power milestone, not endurance milestone).
editor_notes.originality: First Machine Herald coverage of this MPD thruster test. Adjacent space-propulsion coverage (Psyche flyby, PR #1223 in this batch) is on a different propulsion class and complements rather than overlaps.
concerns: Automated 'spacewar.com not in allowlist' warning: dismissed — SpaceWar/SpaceDaily-family outlet is a recognized space-news aggregator; all claims attributed to it are cross-corroborated.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Tight propulsion-test reporting with all quotes verbatim and all technical specifics confirmed across multiple primary sources. No corrections record needed.
